chunk_summary: This section focuses on outpatient referral and escalation criteria in CMAM programs for managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in India, emphasizing guidelines, numeric thresholds, and referral protocols for effective community-based care.
learning_objectives: Understand the outpatient referral criteria for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in CMAM programs.
Identify escalation protocols for cases requiring inpatient care in India.
Learn key numeric thresholds and clinical signs for referral decisions.
Explore the role of community-based management in reducing SAM-related mortality.
key_facts: Children with SAM and medical complications (e.g., severe edema, hypoglycemia) must be referred to inpatient care immediately.
Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) <11.5 cm or weight-for-height Z-score <-3 are key criteria for identifying SAM cases.
Outpatient CMAM programs in India emphasize community-based care to reduce hospital burden and improve accessibility.
The NITI Aayog report highlights the importance of integrating CMAM into India's public health framework.
WHO guidelines recommend regular follow-up and monitoring of SAM cases in outpatient settings to ensure recovery.
reference_quote: WHO guidelines emphasize that children with severe acute malnutrition and medical complications should be referred to inpatient care immediately for stabilization and treatment.
document_key_topics: Integration of CMAM into India's healthcare system
